title: Phase 05 — Generative AI and Large Language Models
prerequisites: Basic understanding of neural networks and Transformers
learningObjectives: Explain what Generative AI is and how it differs from classic AI tasks
Understand what an LLM is and why next-token prediction works
Use key LLM concepts like context window, temperature, tokens, and hallucinations
Get a practical overview of major model families used today
Understand pre-training, fine-tuning, and RLHF conceptually

sections.title: What you will learn in this phase
sections.content:
## What you will learn in this phase:

- What Generative AI creates and how it differs from old prediction-only AI.
- What an LLM is and how it generates fluent text.
- Important model controls: context window, temperature, and token cost.
- The strengths of GPT, Claude, Gemini, Llama, and Mistral at a student level.
- How large models are trained in three major stages.

In plain words: this phase helps you understand what powers ChatGPT-like systems.

Quick Check: Can you explain why token count matters both for quality and cost?

sections.title: Phase 05 Core Concepts (5.1 to 5.5)
sections.content:
## 5.1 — What is Generative AI?

**Analogy:** A normal calculator gives one fixed answer for a formula. A creative writer gives you a brand-new paragraph every time.

Generative AI (AI that creates new content like text, images, code, audio, or video) produces original output patterns based on learned data.

Classic predictive AI often answers classification questions like spam/not-spam. Generative AI can write the full email reply itself.

In plain words: generative AI creates content, not just labels.

Quick Check: Which is generative: "classify this review" or "write a review summary"?

## 5.2 — What is an LLM (Large Language Model)?

**Analogy:** Imagine a person who has read huge amounts of books, websites, and code, and plays "complete the sentence" very accurately.

An LLM (a very large neural language model trained on massive text corpora) predicts the next token based on previous tokens.

- "Large" usually means very high parameter count (model weights learned during training).
- Repeating next-token prediction step-by-step generates full paragraphs and conversations.

In plain words: an LLM is a large pattern engine for language continuation.

Quick Check: Why does repeating next-token prediction produce long coherent text?

## 5.3 — Key concepts every developer must know

### Context window
Context window (the maximum text tokens a model can process in one request) is like working memory.

Longer context allows bigger documents and longer conversations in one call.

In plain words: context window decides how much the model can "see" at once.

Quick Check: What happens if your prompt + docs exceed the context window?

### Temperature
Temperature (a randomness control in sampling) affects output creativity.

- Low temperature: more predictable and stable output.
- High temperature: more varied and creative output.

In plain words: temperature controls how adventurous model word choices are.

Quick Check: For legal-policy drafting, should temperature be low or high?

### Top-p / Top-k
Top-p and Top-k (sampling filters over probable next-token candidates) are additional randomness controls.

They help control diversity while avoiding very unlikely words.

In plain words: top-p/top-k control how wide the model looks before choosing next token.

Quick Check: Why might a product team tune top-p along with temperature?

### Hallucination
Hallucination (confident but incorrect model output) happens because LLMs optimize for likely language patterns, not guaranteed truth.

Mitigations include grounding and retrieval-based workflows such as RAG.

In plain words: fluent output is not always factual output.

Quick Check: What is one practical way to reduce hallucinations in domain Q&A?

### Tokens and cost
Tokens (small text units used for model processing and billing) drive both context usage and price.

A practical approximation: 1 token is around 0.75 words in English, though it varies.

In plain words: more input and output tokens usually means higher cost.

Quick Check: Why should developers estimate token usage before launching a feature?

## 5.4 — Major LLMs available today (honest student overview)

- GPT-4/4o (OpenAI): widely adopted, strong general capability.
- Claude family (Anthropic): strong reasoning and safety-focused design, long-context options.
- Gemini family (Google): strong ecosystem integration, good for Google-native workflows.
- Llama family (Meta): open-weight options suitable for local and custom deployments.
- Mistral models: efficient open models with strong performance-cost balance.

You will use Claude API hands-on in a later phase and Gemini/ADK in the production phase.

In plain words: each model family has strengths; pick based on task, latency, cost, and deployment needs.

Quick Check: When might an open-weight model be preferred over a hosted closed model?

## 5.5 — How LLMs are trained (conceptual only)

**Analogy:** First you read many books, then you do focused practice, then teachers give feedback to improve behavior.

### Step 1 — Pre-training
Pre-training (large-scale next-token learning on huge datasets) teaches broad language patterns.

### Step 2 — Fine-tuning
Fine-tuning (additional training on narrower, high-quality task data) adapts behavior for specific needs.

### Step 3 — RLHF / preference tuning
RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback, where humans rate outputs and model behavior is improved accordingly) aligns responses to be more helpful and safer.

Anthropic also uses Constitutional AI style alignment (principle-guided model behavior shaping).

In plain words: models first learn language broadly, then get task adaptation and alignment.

Quick Check: Why is fine-tuning usually much cheaper than pre-training from scratch?

## What comes next:

In Phase 06, you will learn prompt engineering deeply so you can reliably get better answers from these models in real applications.
